articles.headline: India Unveils 'Green Hydrogen Hubs' Policy to Boost Domestic Production and Exports
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) has launched a new policy framework under the National Green Hydrogen Mission.
• The policy aims to designate specific industrial clusters as 'Green Hydrogen Hubs' to foster integrated production and consumption ecosystems.
• Incentives include tax breaks, subsidized land, and priority access to renewable energy for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
• The move seeks to position India as a global leader in green hydrogen, focusing on reducing carbon emissions and achieving energy independence.
• Special emphasis is placed on developing infrastructure for storage, transport, and export of green hydrogen and its derivatives like green ammonia.
gsSubTopics: Energy
Infrastructure: Energy
Environmental pollution & degradation
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission launched in 2023 with an outlay of ₹19,744 crore.
SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme is a major component, supporting electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
Green Hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water using renewable energy.
India aims to produce 5 million tonnes of green hydrogen annually by 2030.
Policy focuses on 'Green Hydrogen Hubs' for integrated ecosystem development.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's strategy for achieving energy independence and mitigating climate change through green hydrogen.
Analyze the economic and environmental benefits of developing 'Green Hydrogen Hubs' and their potential for job creation and exports.
Examine the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production, including cost, infrastructure, and technological advancements.
Evaluate the role of government policies and incentives in fostering a competitive green hydrogen ecosystem in India.
Assess the geopolitical implications of India becoming a major green hydrogen producer and exporter.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of a green hydrogen production facility with solar panels and wind turbines in the background.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Clarifies Scope of 'Right to be Forgotten' under Digital Personal Data Protection Act
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark ruling, provided clarity on the 'Right to be Forgotten' as enshrined in the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
• The judgment emphasizes balancing an individual's right to privacy with public interest, freedom of speech, and the right to information.
• It specifies criteria for when personal data can be de-listed or delinked from public search results, particularly concerning sensitive personal data and historical offenses.
• The court highlighted the role of the Data Protection Board of India in assessing such requests on a case-by-case basis.
• This ruling is expected to significantly impact digital platforms and data fiduciaries in managing user data and content.
gsSubTopics: Judiciary
Fundamental Rights
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
IT & Computers
Cyber Security
keyTerms: Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act)
Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, includes provisions for the 'Right to Erasure' and 'Right to Correction and Completion'.
The 'Right to be Forgotten' is an aspect of the right to privacy, affirmed by the Supreme Court in K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017).
Data Fiduciary is an entity determining the purpose and means of processing personal data.
Data Principal is the individual to whom the personal data relates.
The Data Protection Board of India is established under the DPDP Act for enforcement.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's ruling on the 'Right to be Forgotten' for individuals and digital platforms.
Discuss the challenges in balancing the right to privacy with freedom of expression and public interest in the digital age.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, in safeguarding fundamental rights.
Examine the role of the judiciary in interpreting and enforcing data protection laws in India.
Suggest measures to improve digital literacy and awareness about data rights among citizens.
articles.imageDescription: Gavel on top of court documents with a digital data network overlay.
articles.trivia: The 'Right to be Forgotten' originated in Europe with a 2014 European Court of Justice ruling against Google.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Easing Inflationary Pressures, Focus on Growth
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) unanimously decided to keep the repo rate unchanged at 6.5% for the sixth consecutive review.
• This decision comes as retail inflation shows signs of moderation, albeit remaining above the MPC's comfort zone, while global economic uncertainties persist.
• The RBI reiterated its commitment to 'withdrawal of accommodation' to ensure inflation aligns with the target while supporting economic growth.
• GDP growth projection for FY26 was revised marginally upwards, signaling cautious optimism regarding economic recovery.
• The MPC highlighted the importance of a stable financial system and continues to monitor liquidity conditions closely.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary Policy
Inflation
Growth
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) consists of six members: three from RBI and three external members appointed by the government.
The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Retail inflation target is 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
Withdrawal of accommodation refers to reducing surplus liquidity in the system and raising interest rates to curb inflation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind the RBI's decision to maintain the status quo on the repo rate and its implications for the Indian economy.
Discuss the challenges faced by the MPC in balancing inflation control with the objective of fostering economic growth.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the RBI's monetary policy tools in managing liquidity and credit conditions.
Examine the impact of global economic uncertainties and geopolitical developments on India's monetary policy decisions.
Suggest reforms to strengthen the autonomy and effectiveness of the Monetary Policy Committee.
articles.imageDescription: RBI logo with graphs showing economic trends in the background.
articles.trivia: The RBI was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934, and was nationalized in 1949.

articles.headline: India-France 10th Strategic Dialogue Emphasizes Defence Cooperation and Indo-Pacific Security
articles.explainer:
• India and France concluded their 10th Strategic Dialogue in New Delhi, co-chaired by the National Security Advisors of both nations.
• Discussions focused on deepening defence industrial collaboration, including co-development and co-production of advanced military platforms.
• Both sides reiterated their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, agreeing to enhance maritime security cooperation and information sharing.
• The dialogue also covered counter-terrorism efforts, cyber security, and cooperation in space and civil nuclear energy.
• This annual dialogue underscores the robust and comprehensive strategic partnership between the two countries, celebrating 25 years of strategic partnership.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
India and its neighbourhood- relations
prelimsPoints: India and France established their strategic partnership in 1998.
The dialogue is typically led by the National Security Advisors (NSAs) of both countries.
France is a key defence partner for India, known for Rafale jets and Scorpene submarines.
Both nations are committed to a rules-based order in the Indo-Pacific region.
Areas of cooperation include defence, space, nuclear energy, and cybersecurity.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the India-France strategic partnership in India's foreign policy and defence strategy.
Discuss how cooperation with France contributes to India's objective of achieving strategic autonomy and strengthening its defence industrial base.
Evaluate the role of India and France in ensuring security and stability in the Indo-Pacific region.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in deepening defence and technological collaboration between the two countries.
Suggest ways to expand the bilateral relationship beyond traditional areas into new domains like climate change and digital technology.
articles.imageDescription: National Security Advisors of India and France shaking hands during the dialogue.
articles.trivia: France was one of the first countries to establish a strategic partnership with India after its nuclear tests in 1998.

articles.headline: National Cyber Security Strategy 2.0 Focuses on Critical Infrastructure Protection and Skill Development
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) unveiled the National Cyber Security Strategy 2.0, updating the previous framework from 2013.
• The new strategy prioritizes securing critical information infrastructure (CII) across sectors like energy, finance, and telecommunications.
• It emphasizes building a robust cyber workforce through skill development programs and public-private partnerships.
• Key pillars include active cyber defence, resilience building, incident response mechanisms, and international cooperation.
• The strategy also addresses emerging threats from AI, quantum computing, and supply chain vulnerabilities, aiming for a 'secure and resilient' cyberspace.
gsSubTopics: Internal security challenges
Cyber security
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
articles.source: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY)
keyTerms: NCIIPC (National Critical Information Infrastructure Protection Centre)
prelimsPoints: The first National Cyber Security Policy was launched in 2013.
CERT-In is the national agency for incident response and threat intelligence.
NCIIPC is responsible for protecting critical information infrastructure.
The new strategy addresses emerging technologies like AI, IoT, and quantum computing.
Focus areas include supply chain security, cyber deterrence, and international collaboration.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key features and objectives of India's National Cyber Security Strategy 2.0 and its significance in the evolving digital landscape.
Discuss the challenges in protecting critical information infrastructure and the measures proposed in the strategy to address them.
Evaluate the role of public-private partnerships and skill development in building a robust cyber workforce and enhancing national cyber resilience.
Examine the implications of emerging technologies like AI and quantum computing on cybersecurity and India's preparedness.
Suggest policy recommendations for strengthening international cooperation in combating trans-border cyber threats.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract image of a digital shield protecting a network grid.
articles.trivia: India ranks among the top 10 countries globally in the ITU's Global Cybersecurity Index.

articles.headline: NCAP Phase-II: Government Launches New Initiatives for City-Specific Air Pollution Reduction Targets
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) has inaugurated Phase-II of the National Clean Air Programme (NCAP).
• This phase introduces more stringent, city-specific targets for reducing particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10) concentrations by 20-30% by 2029, building on earlier goals.
• New initiatives include enhanced deployment of advanced air quality monitoring stations and real-time data dissemination platforms.
• Focus will be on source apportionment studies in additional non-attainment cities and promoting electric vehicles and clean energy.
• The program aims for a holistic approach, integrating urban planning, industrial emission control, and waste management strategies.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
articles.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
keyTerms: System of Air Quality and Weather Forecasting and Research (SAFAR)
prelimsPoints: NCAP was launched in 2019 to reduce particulate matter concentration by 20-30% by 2024 from 2017 levels.
It covers 131 non-attainment cities across India.
PM2.5 and PM10 are key indicators of air quality.
Source apportionment studies identify major contributors to air pollution in a specific area.
SAFAR provides real-time air quality information and forecasts.
mainsPoints: Assess the effectiveness of NCAP Phase-I in improving urban air quality and the lessons learned for Phase-II.
Discuss the multi-faceted challenges in combating air pollution in Indian cities, including vehicular emissions, industrial discharge, and agricultural burning.
Evaluate the new initiatives under NCAP Phase-II, such as city-specific targets and advanced monitoring, for their potential impact.
Examine the role of inter-ministerial coordination and public participation in achieving clean air goals.
Suggest sustainable urban planning and policy interventions required to address the root causes of air pollution.
articles.imageDescription: City skyline shrouded in smog with a clean air technology superimposed.
articles.trivia: Globally, air pollution is responsible for millions of premature deaths each year, making it one of the largest environmental health risks.

articles.headline: India Pitches for Global Framework on Ethical AI Governance at UN General Assembly Side Event
articles.explainer:
• India, at a side event during the 80th UN General Assembly session, advocated for a robust international framework for ethical AI governance.
• The Indian delegation highlighted the need for AI development to be responsible, inclusive, and transparent, ensuring it benefits all humanity.
• Discussions focused on mitigating risks associated with AI, such as algorithmic bias, privacy violations, and autonomous weapons systems.
• India emphasized its 'AI for All' approach, promoting equitable access and capacity building for developing nations.
• The initiative seeks to build consensus among member states on common principles and norms for AI regulation and deployment.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
IT & Computers
Important International Institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
prelimsPoints: UN General Assembly's 80th session is ongoing in September 2025.
India has been advocating for multi-stakeholder and democratic governance of emerging technologies.
Ethical AI principles include fairness, accountability, transparency, and privacy.
Algorithmic bias refers to systematic and repeatable errors in a computer system's output that create unfair outcomes.
India's national AI strategy promotes 'AI for All' for inclusive growth.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's rationale for advocating a global framework on ethical AI governance and its potential impact on international relations.
Discuss the key ethical challenges posed by Artificial Intelligence, including bias, privacy, and accountability.
Evaluate the 'AI for All' approach and its relevance for developing countries in harnessing AI benefits while mitigating risks.
Examine the role of international organizations like the UN in shaping norms and regulations for emerging technologies.
Suggest how India can leverage its technological expertise and diplomatic influence to lead global discussions on responsible AI development.
articles.imageDescription: Globe with interconnected AI neural networks, symbolizing global governance.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined in 1956 by John McCarthy.

articles.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Reports Significant Progress in Health ID Creation and Telemedicine Services
articles.explainer:
• The National Health Authority (NHA) released its annual progress report for the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM), highlighting major achievements.
• Over 700 million unique Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA) IDs have been generated, facilitating digital access to health records.
• The mission has significantly expanded its network of healthcare facilities, including hospitals, clinics, and diagnostic labs, linked to the ABHA platform.
• Telemedicine services under eSanjeevani have seen a surge in consultations, improving access to healthcare in remote areas.
• The report emphasizes improved data interoperability and digital health service delivery, contributing to universal health coverage.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Health, Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: ABDM was launched nationwide in September 2021.
ABHA ID is a 14-digit unique health identifier.
eSanjeevani is a national telemedicine service operated by the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare.
NHA is the implementing agency for ABDM and Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB PM-JAY).
The mission aims to create a seamless online platform for interoperability of digital health records.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the progress and impact of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission in transforming India's healthcare landscape.
Discuss the benefits of digital health IDs and telemedicine services in improving healthcare access, efficiency, and equity.
Examine the challenges faced in the implementation of ABDM, including data privacy concerns, digital literacy gaps, and infrastructure limitations.
Analyze how ABDM aligns with the goal of achieving Universal Health Coverage and strengthening the public health system.
Suggest measures to further enhance the adoption and effectiveness of digital health initiatives in India.
articles.imageDescription: People using digital devices for health consultations, with the ABHA logo.
articles.trivia: India's National Health Policy 2017 envisages the 'highest possible level of health and well-being for all at all ages'.

articles.headline: Delimitation Commission Submits Interim Report on Boundary Adjustments in Northeast States
articles.explainer:
• The Delimitation Commission, constituted for select North-Eastern states, has submitted an interim report to the President of India.
• The report proposes adjustments to assembly and parliamentary constituencies based on the latest census data and geographical considerations.
• The exercise aims to ensure equitable representation for various segments of the population and geographical areas.
• Stakeholder consultations were held with political parties, civil society groups, and local administrations to gather feedback.
• The final report is expected to be crucial for the upcoming state assembly elections in the region, impacting political dynamics.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions
prelimsPoints: Delimitation is the act or process of fixing limits or boundaries of territorial constituencies in a country or a province having a legislative body.
The Delimitation Commission is appointed by the President of India and works in collaboration with the Election Commission of India.
Its orders have the force of law and cannot be challenged in any court.
The last major delimitation exercise in India was completed in 2008.
The next delimitation is frozen until the first census after 2026.
mainsPoints: Discuss the constitutional provisions and objectives behind the delimitation exercise in India.
Analyze the political and social implications of delimitation, particularly in ethnically diverse regions like the Northeast.
Evaluate the challenges faced by the Delimitation Commission in ensuring fair and equitable representation.
Examine the arguments for and against freezing delimitation until 2026 and its impact on demographic changes.
Suggest measures to make the delimitation process more transparent and acceptable to all stakeholders.
articles.imageDescription: Map of Northeast India with demarcated electoral constituencies.
articles.trivia: The first Delimitation Commission was constituted in 1952.

articles.headline: Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme Extended to Cover Advanced Battery Manufacturing and Textiles
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved the expansion of the Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme to include new sectors: Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC) Battery Manufacturing and Textiles.
• This move aims to boost domestic manufacturing capabilities, attract global investments, and enhance India's export competitiveness in these strategic sectors.
• For ACC batteries, the scheme targets reducing import dependence for electric vehicles and energy storage solutions.
• In textiles, the PLI scheme focuses on promoting the production of Man-Made Fibre (MMF) apparel and technical textiles.
• The expansion is part of the 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiative, designed to integrate India into global supply chains and create employment opportunities.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth
Infrastructure: Energy
prelimsPoints: The PLI scheme was introduced in 2020 to boost domestic manufacturing and exports.
It provides incentives on incremental sales for products manufactured in India.
Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC) batteries are crucial for electric vehicles and grid-scale energy storage.
Technical textiles are functional fabrics used in diverse industries like automotive, medical, and agriculture.
The scheme currently covers 14 key sectors, with these additions making it 16.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and expected outcomes of extending the PLI scheme to Advanced Battery Manufacturing and Textiles.
Discuss how the PLI scheme contributes to India's 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' vision and its goal of becoming a global manufacturing hub.
Evaluate the potential challenges and opportunities for these new sectors under the PLI framework.
Examine the fiscal implications of the PLI scheme and its impact on government finances and industrial growth.
Suggest measures to enhance the effectiveness and broaden the scope of the PLI scheme to other critical sectors.
articles.imageDescription: Factory floor with robots assembling battery cells, and a textile loom in the background.
articles.trivia: India is the world's second-largest producer of textiles and garments.

articles.headline: India Calls for Comprehensive UNSC Reforms and Equitable Global Order at UNGA 80
articles.explainer:
• India's External Affairs Minister, addressing the 80th session of the UN General Assembly (UNGA), strongly advocated for urgent reforms of the UN Security Council (UNSC).
• He highlighted the anachronistic nature of the current UNSC structure, which does not reflect contemporary global realities.
• India reiterated its legitimate claim for a permanent seat in an expanded Security Council, alongside other developing nations.
• The Minister emphasized the need for a more equitable and representative global order to effectively address complex challenges like climate change, pandemics, and terrorism.
• He also called for greater multilateralism and solidarity among nations to uphold international law and peace.
gsSubTopics: Important International Institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The UNSC has 5 permanent members (P5) and 10 non-permanent members elected for two-year terms.
India has served multiple terms as a non-permanent member of the UNSC.
The G4 nations (India, Brazil, Germany, Japan) mutually support each other's bids for permanent seats.
UNGA is the main deliberative, policymaking and representative organ of the UN.
India has been advocating for the adoption of the Comprehensive Convention on International Terrorism (CCIT) since 1996.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's persistent demand for UN Security Council reforms and the rationale behind its claim for a permanent seat.
Discuss the challenges and obstacles in achieving consensus on UNSC reforms among member states.
Evaluate the implications of an unreformed UNSC for global peace, security, and multilateral governance.
Examine how a more equitable and representative global order, as advocated by India, could better address contemporary international challenges.
Suggest diplomatic strategies India can employ to garner broader support for its UNSC permanent membership bid.
articles.imageDescription: UN General Assembly hall with a speaker at the podium.
articles.trivia: The UN Charter was signed on 26 June 1945, in San Francisco, at the conclusion of the United Nations Conference on International Organization.

articles.headline: New Study Highlights Accelerated Retreat of Himalayan Glaciers, Urges Urgent Climate Action
articles.explainer:
• A comprehensive study published by the Wadia Institute of Himalayan Geology reveals an alarming rate of retreat for glaciers across the Himalayan region.
• The research, utilizing satellite imagery and field data, indicates that glaciers are melting at twice the rate observed in the late 20th century.
• This accelerated melting poses severe threats to water security for millions in South Asia, increases the risk of Glacier Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs), and impacts regional ecosystems.
• Scientists attribute the rapid retreat primarily to rising global temperatures and localized black carbon deposition.
• The study calls for immediate and concerted efforts in climate change mitigation and adaptation strategies across the Himalayan nations.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution & degradation
Disaster and disaster management

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes
prelimsPoints: The Himalayas are often called the 'Third Pole' due to their vast ice reserves outside the polar regions.
Glacier Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) are sudden releases of water from glacier lakes.
Black carbon, a component of particulate matter, accelerates glacier melt by absorbing sunlight.
The Indus, Ganga, and Brahmaputra rivers are fed by Himalayan glaciers.
The Wadia Institute of Himalayan Geology is an autonomous research organization under the Department of Science & Technology, Govt. of India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the causes and consequences of accelerated Himalayan glacier retreat on water resources, ecosystems, and human livelihoods in South Asia.
Discuss the immediate and long-term risks posed by Glacier Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) and the strategies for disaster risk reduction.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current climate change mitigation and adaptation policies in protecting the Himalayan region.
Examine the role of regional cooperation among Himalayan nations in addressing transboundary impacts of glacier melt.
Suggest sustainable development practices and policy interventions to build resilience in communities dependent on Himalayan ecosystems.
articles.imageDescription: Melting glacier in the Himalayas with visible water runoff.
articles.trivia: The Himalayas contain the world's third-largest deposit of ice and snow, after the Arctic and Antarctic.

articles.headline: IIT-Bombay Researchers Develop Novel Solid-State Battery for Enhanced Renewable Energy Storage
articles.explainer:
• Scientists at IIT-Bombay have announced a significant breakthrough in battery technology with the development of a novel solid-state battery.
• The new battery chemistry promises higher energy density, faster charging capabilities, and enhanced safety compared to conventional lithium-ion batteries.
• This innovation is particularly geared towards efficient storage solutions for renewable energy sources like solar and wind power, and for electric vehicles.
• The research focuses on using indigenous materials, reducing dependence on critical imported raw materials.
• The team is now working on scaling up the technology for commercial production and integration into India's energy ecosystem.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Infrastructure: Energy
prelimsPoints: Solid-state batteries use a solid electrolyte instead of the liquid or polymer gel electrolyte found in conventional lithium-ion batteries.
They offer advantages like higher energy density, longer lifespan, and improved safety (less prone to thermal runaway).
Lithium-ion batteries are widely used in portable electronics and electric vehicles.
Indigenous development of battery technology is crucial for India's energy security and EV roadmap.
IIT Bombay is one of India's premier technical institutes.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of solid-state battery technology for India's energy transition and electric vehicle ecosystem.
Analyze the advantages and challenges of developing and commercializing indigenous battery technologies.
Evaluate the potential impact of this breakthrough on renewable energy storage and grid stability.
Examine the role of academic research and innovation in addressing critical technological needs for national development.
Suggest policy measures to facilitate the translation of laboratory breakthroughs into large-scale industrial applications.
articles.imageDescription: Microscopic view of battery components with energy flow lines.
articles.trivia: The first battery, the voltaic pile, was invented by Alessandro Volta in 1800.

articles.headline: Ministry of Tribal Affairs Launches Scheme to Preserve Endangered Tribal Languages and Oral Traditions
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tribal Affairs has initiated a new scheme, 'Aadi Bhasha Sarankshan Abhiyan', focusing on the preservation and promotion of endangered tribal languages.
• The program aims to document, digitize, and revitalize tribal languages through community participation and technological interventions.
• It includes initiatives for developing educational materials in tribal languages and training local youth as linguistic resource persons.
• The scheme recognizes the rich cultural heritage embedded in these languages, including oral traditions, folklore, and indigenous knowledge systems.
• This effort is part of a broader commitment to protect India's linguistic diversity and empower tribal communities.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Social empowerment
prelimsPoints: India is home to hundreds of tribal languages, many of which are endangered.
UNESCO's Atlas of the World's Languages in Danger lists many Indian languages.
Article 29 of the Indian Constitution protects the interests of minorities, including linguistic minorities.
The scheme emphasizes community-led initiatives and digital documentation.
The Ministry of Tribal Affairs is the nodal ministry for tribal welfare.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of preserving endangered tribal languages for India's cultural heritage and linguistic diversity.
Analyze the factors contributing to the endangerment of tribal languages and their socio-cultural implications.
Evaluate the 'Aadi Bhasha Sarankshan Abhiyan' scheme's approach and its potential for revitalizing tribal languages.
Examine the challenges in implementing language preservation programs, including resource allocation and community engagement.
Suggest comprehensive strategies, including policy and educational reforms, to protect and promote India's linguistic heritage.
articles.imageDescription: Elderly tribal members sharing stories with younger generations, symbolizing oral tradition.
articles.trivia: The 'People's Linguistic Survey of India' has documented over 780 languages in India.

articles.headline: Parliamentary Panel Continues Consultations on Uniform Civil Code, Advocates for Broader Public Discourse
articles.explainer:
• A parliamentary standing committee resumed its consultations on the Uniform Civil Code (UCC), inviting inputs from various stakeholders.
• The committee emphasized the need for a comprehensive and inclusive public discourse to build consensus on this sensitive issue.
• Deliberations focused on the constitutional mandate for UCC under Article 44 and its potential implications for personal laws and gender justice.
• Representatives from religious organizations, legal experts, and women's rights groups presented diverse viewpoints.
• The panel aims to prepare a detailed report synthesizing all perspectives before making recommendations for legislative action.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Directive Principles of State Policy
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these
prelimsPoints: Article 44 of the Constitution states: 'The State shall endeavour to secure for the citizens a Uniform Civil Code throughout the territory of India.'
UCC falls under Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP), which are non-justiciable.
Personal laws govern matters like marriage, divorce, inheritance, and adoption for different religious communities.
Goa is the only Indian state with a Uniform Civil Code.
The Law Commission of India has previously examined the feasibility of UCC.
mainsPoints: Discuss the constitutional mandate and historical context of the Uniform Civil Code in India.
Analyze the arguments for and against the implementation of UCC, considering its implications for religious freedom and national integration.
Evaluate how a UCC could contribute to gender justice and equality, while addressing concerns of minority communities.
Examine the challenges in drafting and implementing a UCC that is acceptable to diverse religious and cultural groups.
Suggest a roadmap for building consensus and facilitating a gradual approach towards a Uniform Civil Code in India.
articles.imageDescription: Indian Parliament building with a focus on legal documents.
articles.trivia: During the Constituent Assembly debates, B.R. Ambedkar advocated for a UCC, seeing it as a desirable goal for national unity.

highlights: India launches 'Green Hydrogen Hubs' policy to boost domestic production and global exports, aiming for energy independence.
Supreme Court clarifies the 'Right to be Forgotten' under Digital Personal Data Protection Act, balancing privacy with public interest.
RBI's Monetary Policy Committee maintains status quo on repo rate, focusing on growth amidst easing inflation.
India-France 10th Strategic Dialogue emphasizes deeper defence industrial cooperation and Indo-Pacific security.
National Cyber Security Strategy 2.0 unveiled, prioritizing critical infrastructure protection and cyber skill development.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Green Hydrogen Mission, consider the following statements:
1. It aims to make India a global hub for the production and export of green hydrogen.
2. The SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme is a component focusing solely on green hydrogen production.
3. Green hydrogen is produced by the electrolysis of water using renewable energy sources.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The mission aims to position India as a global leader. Statement 2 is incorrect. The SIGHT programme supports both electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production. Statement 3 is correct. Green hydrogen is defined by its production method using renewable energy for electrolysis.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following bodies is responsible for implementing the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM)?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The National Health Authority (NHA) is the implementing agency for the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) and Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB PM-JAY).

prelimsMocks.question: In the context of India's National Cyber Security Strategy 2.0, what does 'CII' primarily refer to?
prelimsMocks.explanation: CII stands for Critical Information Infrastructure, which refers to the assets, systems, and networks whose continuous operation is deemed essential to the security of a country, economy, or public health and safety.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Uniform Civil Code (UCC) in India:
1. Article 44 of the Indian Constitution mandates the State to endeavour to secure a UCC.
2. The UCC falls under the Fundamental Rights chapter of the Constitution.
3. Goa is the only Indian state currently having a Uniform Civil Code.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. Article 44 is a Directive Principle of State Policy. Statement 2 is incorrect. UCC falls under Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV), not Fundamental Rights (Part III). Statement 3 is correct. Goa has a common family law for all its religious communities.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following is NOT a primary objective of the Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The PLI scheme aims to reduce import dependence by boosting domestic manufacturing and making Indian industries globally competitive. Increasing import dependence is contrary to its objectives.

mainsMocks.question: Discuss the multi-faceted challenges in implementing India's National Green Hydrogen Mission and suggest a comprehensive roadmap for achieving its ambitious targets. (150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Indian Economy; Energy; Environmental pollution & degradation
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the mission's objective. Identify key challenges: high production costs, infrastructure development (storage, transport), technological gaps, policy consistency, and financing. Propose a roadmap: R&D focus, PLI-like incentives, dedicated infrastructure corridors, international partnerships, and skill development.

mainsMocks.question: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's clarification on the 'Right to be Forgotten' under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, for individual privacy and freedom of expression in the digital age. (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Fundamental Rights; Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors; GS3: Cyber Security
mainsMocks.approach: Begin by defining 'Right to be Forgotten' and its origin. Explain the DPDP Act's provisions. Analyze how the SC ruling balances individual privacy (erasure of outdated/irrelevant data) against public interest (historical records, journalistic freedom, public safety). Discuss challenges in implementation for digital platforms, potential for misuse, and the role of the Data Protection Board. Conclude on the evolving jurisprudence of digital rights.

mainsMocks.question: The persistent demand for UN Security Council reforms by India reflects a changing global order. Critically examine the arguments for and against expanding the UNSC's permanent membership and India's role in this discourse. (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Important International Institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate; Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the current UNSC structure and India's demand for reform. Argue for expansion: reflects current geopolitical realities, enhances legitimacy, includes emerging powers (G4), and improves decision-making. Argue against: P5 reluctance, difficulty in defining new criteria, potential for more veto powers, and increased bureaucracy. Discuss India's active role, its contributions to UN peacekeeping, and its democratic credentials. Conclude on the need for a pragmatic approach to multilateralism.

quickBytes.text: The 'Right to be Forgotten' is an aspect of the Right to Privacy under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution.
The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) in India has a target to maintain retail inflation at 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
India and France established their strategic partnership in 1998, focusing on defence, space, and nuclear energy.
CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team) is the national nodal agency for responding to computer security incidents.
The National Clean Air Programme (NCAP) was launched in 2019 to reduce particulate matter pollution across Indian cities.
Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA) ID is a 14-digit unique health identifier under the ABDM.
The Delimitation Commission's orders have the force of law and cannot be challenged in any court of law.
Goa is the only Indian state that currently has a Uniform Civil Code (UCC).
Glacier Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) are a significant hazard in the Himalayan region due to climate change.
Technical textiles are functional fabrics used in diverse industries, including automotive, medical, and agriculture.

GS1.headline: Ministry of Tribal Affairs Launches Scheme to Preserve Endangered Tribal Languages and Oral Traditions
GS1.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tribal Affairs has initiated a new scheme, 'Aadi Bhasha Sarankshan Abhiyan', focusing on the preservation and promotion of endangered tribal languages.
• The program aims to document, digitize, and revitalize tribal languages through community participation and technological interventions.
• It includes initiatives for developing educational materials in tribal languages and training local youth as linguistic resource persons.
• The scheme recognizes the rich cultural heritage embedded in these languages, including oral traditions, folklore, and indigenous knowledge systems.
• This effort is part of a broader commitment to protect India's linguistic diversity and empower tribal communities.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Social empowerment
prelimsPoints: India is home to hundreds of tribal languages, many of which are endangered.
UNESCO's Atlas of the World's Languages in Danger lists many Indian languages.
Article 29 of the Indian Constitution protects the interests of minorities, including linguistic minorities.
The scheme emphasizes community-led initiatives and digital documentation.
The Ministry of Tribal Affairs is the nodal ministry for tribal welfare.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of preserving endangered tribal languages for India's cultural heritage and linguistic diversity.
Analyze the factors contributing to the endangerment of tribal languages and their socio-cultural implications.
Evaluate the 'Aadi Bhasha Sarankshan Abhiyan' scheme's approach and its potential for revitalizing tribal languages.
Examine the challenges in implementing language preservation programs, including resource allocation and community engagement.
Suggest comprehensive strategies, including policy and educational reforms, to protect and promote India's linguistic heritage.
GS1.imageDescription: Elderly tribal members sharing stories with younger generations, symbolizing oral tradition.
GS1.trivia: The 'People's Linguistic Survey of India' has documented over 780 languages in India.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Clarifies Scope of 'Right to be Forgotten' under Digital Personal Data Protection Act
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark ruling, provided clarity on the 'Right to be Forgotten' as enshrined in the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
• The judgment emphasizes balancing an individual's right to privacy with public interest, freedom of speech, and the right to information.
• It specifies criteria for when personal data can be de-listed or delinked from public search results, particularly concerning sensitive personal data and historical offenses.
• The court highlighted the role of the Data Protection Board of India in assessing such requests on a case-by-case basis.
• This ruling is expected to significantly impact digital platforms and data fiduciaries in managing user data and content.
gsSubTopics: Judiciary
Fundamental Rights
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
IT & Computers
Cyber Security
keyTerms: Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act)
Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, includes provisions for the 'Right to Erasure' and 'Right to Correction and Completion'.
The 'Right to be Forgotten' is an aspect of the right to privacy, affirmed by the Supreme Court in K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017).
Data Fiduciary is an entity determining the purpose and means of processing personal data.
Data Principal is the individual to whom the personal data relates.
The Data Protection Board of India is established under the DPDP Act for enforcement.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's ruling on the 'Right to be Forgotten' for individuals and digital platforms.
Discuss the challenges in balancing the right to privacy with freedom of expression and public interest in the digital age.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, in safeguarding fundamental rights.
Examine the role of the judiciary in interpreting and enforcing data protection laws in India.
Suggest measures to improve digital literacy and awareness about data rights among citizens.
GS2.imageDescription: Gavel on top of court documents with a digital data network overlay.
GS2.trivia: The 'Right to be Forgotten' originated in Europe with a 2014 European Court of Justice ruling against Google.

GS2.headline: India-France 10th Strategic Dialogue Emphasizes Defence Cooperation and Indo-Pacific Security
GS2.explainer:
• India and France concluded their 10th Strategic Dialogue in New Delhi, co-chaired by the National Security Advisors of both nations.
• Discussions focused on deepening defence industrial collaboration, including co-development and co-production of advanced military platforms.
• Both sides reiterated their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, agreeing to enhance maritime security cooperation and information sharing.
• The dialogue also covered counter-terrorism efforts, cyber security, and cooperation in space and civil nuclear energy.
• This annual dialogue underscores the robust and comprehensive strategic partnership between the two countries, celebrating 25 years of strategic partnership.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
India and its neighbourhood- relations
prelimsPoints: India and France established their strategic partnership in 1998.
The dialogue is typically led by the National Security Advisors (NSAs) of both countries.
France is a key defence partner for India, known for Rafale jets and Scorpene submarines.
Both nations are committed to a rules-based order in the Indo-Pacific region.
Areas of cooperation include defence, space, nuclear energy, and cybersecurity.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the India-France strategic partnership in India's foreign policy and defence strategy.
Discuss how cooperation with France contributes to India's objective of achieving strategic autonomy and strengthening its defence industrial base.
Evaluate the role of India and France in ensuring security and stability in the Indo-Pacific region.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in deepening defence and technological collaboration between the two countries.
Suggest ways to expand the bilateral relationship beyond traditional areas into new domains like climate change and digital technology.
GS2.imageDescription: National Security Advisors of India and France shaking hands during the dialogue.
GS2.trivia: France was one of the first countries to establish a strategic partnership with India after its nuclear tests in 1998.

GS2.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Reports Significant Progress in Health ID Creation and Telemedicine Services
GS2.explainer:
• The National Health Authority (NHA) released its annual progress report for the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM), highlighting major achievements.
• Over 700 million unique Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA) IDs have been generated, facilitating digital access to health records.
• The mission has significantly expanded its network of healthcare facilities, including hospitals, clinics, and diagnostic labs, linked to the ABHA platform.
• Telemedicine services under eSanjeevani have seen a surge in consultations, improving access to healthcare in remote areas.
• The report emphasizes improved data interoperability and digital health service delivery, contributing to universal health coverage.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Health, Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: ABDM was launched nationwide in September 2021.
ABHA ID is a 14-digit unique health identifier.
eSanjeevani is a national telemedicine service operated by the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare.
NHA is the implementing agency for ABDM and Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB PM-JAY).
The mission aims to create a seamless online platform for interoperability of digital health records.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the progress and impact of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission in transforming India's healthcare landscape.
Discuss the benefits of digital health IDs and telemedicine services in improving healthcare access, efficiency, and equity.
Examine the challenges faced in the implementation of ABDM, including data privacy concerns, digital literacy gaps, and infrastructure limitations.
Analyze how ABDM aligns with the goal of achieving Universal Health Coverage and strengthening the public health system.
Suggest measures to further enhance the adoption and effectiveness of digital health initiatives in India.
GS2.imageDescription: People using digital devices for health consultations, with the ABHA logo.
GS2.trivia: India's National Health Policy 2017 envisages the 'highest possible level of health and well-being for all at all ages'.

GS2.headline: Delimitation Commission Submits Interim Report on Boundary Adjustments in Northeast States
GS2.explainer:
• The Delimitation Commission, constituted for select North-Eastern states, has submitted an interim report to the President of India.
• The report proposes adjustments to assembly and parliamentary constituencies based on the latest census data and geographical considerations.
• The exercise aims to ensure equitable representation for various segments of the population and geographical areas.
• Stakeholder consultations were held with political parties, civil society groups, and local administrations to gather feedback.
• The final report is expected to be crucial for the upcoming state assembly elections in the region, impacting political dynamics.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions
prelimsPoints: Delimitation is the act or process of fixing limits or boundaries of territorial constituencies in a country or a province having a legislative body.
The Delimitation Commission is appointed by the President of India and works in collaboration with the Election Commission of India.
Its orders have the force of law and cannot be challenged in any court.
The last major delimitation exercise in India was completed in 2008.
The next delimitation is frozen until the first census after 2026.
mainsPoints: Discuss the constitutional provisions and objectives behind the delimitation exercise in India.
Analyze the political and social implications of delimitation, particularly in ethnically diverse regions like the Northeast.
Evaluate the challenges faced by the Delimitation Commission in ensuring fair and equitable representation.
Examine the arguments for and against freezing delimitation until 2026 and its impact on demographic changes.
Suggest measures to make the delimitation process more transparent and acceptable to all stakeholders.
GS2.imageDescription: Map of Northeast India with demarcated electoral constituencies.
GS2.trivia: The first Delimitation Commission was constituted in 1952.

GS2.headline: India Calls for Comprehensive UNSC Reforms and Equitable Global Order at UNGA 80
GS2.explainer:
• India's External Affairs Minister, addressing the 80th session of the UN General Assembly (UNGA), strongly advocated for urgent reforms of the UN Security Council (UNSC).
• He highlighted the anachronistic nature of the current UNSC structure, which does not reflect contemporary global realities.
• India reiterated its legitimate claim for a permanent seat in an expanded Security Council, alongside other developing nations.
• The Minister emphasized the need for a more equitable and representative global order to effectively address complex challenges like climate change, pandemics, and terrorism.
• He also called for greater multilateralism and solidarity among nations to uphold international law and peace.
gsSubTopics: Important International Institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The UNSC has 5 permanent members (P5) and 10 non-permanent members elected for two-year terms.
India has served multiple terms as a non-permanent member of the UNSC.
The G4 nations (India, Brazil, Germany, Japan) mutually support each other's bids for permanent seats.
UNGA is the main deliberative, policymaking and representative organ of the UN.
India has been advocating for the adoption of the Comprehensive Convention on International Terrorism (CCIT) since 1996.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's persistent demand for UN Security Council reforms and the rationale behind its claim for a permanent seat.
Discuss the challenges and obstacles in achieving consensus on UNSC reforms among member states.
Evaluate the implications of an unreformed UNSC for global peace, security, and multilateral governance.
Examine how a more equitable and representative global order, as advocated by India, could better address contemporary international challenges.
Suggest diplomatic strategies India can employ to garner broader support for its UNSC permanent membership bid.
GS2.imageDescription: UN General Assembly hall with a speaker at the podium.
GS2.trivia: The UN Charter was signed on 26 June 1945, in San Francisco, at the conclusion of the United Nations Conference on International Organization.

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Panel Continues Consultations on Uniform Civil Code, Advocates for Broader Public Discourse
GS2.explainer:
• A parliamentary standing committee resumed its consultations on the Uniform Civil Code (UCC), inviting inputs from various stakeholders.
• The committee emphasized the need for a comprehensive and inclusive public discourse to build consensus on this sensitive issue.
• Deliberations focused on the constitutional mandate for UCC under Article 44 and its potential implications for personal laws and gender justice.
• Representatives from religious organizations, legal experts, and women's rights groups presented diverse viewpoints.
• The panel aims to prepare a detailed report synthesizing all perspectives before making recommendations for legislative action.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Directive Principles of State Policy
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these
prelimsPoints: Article 44 of the Constitution states: 'The State shall endeavour to secure for the citizens a Uniform Civil Code throughout the territory of India.'
UCC falls under Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP), which are non-justiciable.
Personal laws govern matters like marriage, divorce, inheritance, and adoption for different religious communities.
Goa is the only Indian state with a Uniform Civil Code.
The Law Commission of India has previously examined the feasibility of UCC.
mainsPoints: Discuss the constitutional mandate and historical context of the Uniform Civil Code in India.
Analyze the arguments for and against the implementation of UCC, considering its implications for religious freedom and national integration.
Evaluate how a UCC could contribute to gender justice and equality, while addressing concerns of minority communities.
Examine the challenges in drafting and implementing a UCC that is acceptable to diverse religious and cultural groups.
Suggest a roadmap for building consensus and facilitating a gradual approach towards a Uniform Civil Code in India.
GS2.imageDescription: Indian Parliament building with a focus on legal documents.
GS2.trivia: During the Constituent Assembly debates, B.R. Ambedkar advocated for a UCC, seeing it as a desirable goal for national unity.

GS3.headline: India Unveils 'Green Hydrogen Hubs' Policy to Boost Domestic Production and Exports
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) has launched a new policy framework under the National Green Hydrogen Mission.
• The policy aims to designate specific industrial clusters as 'Green Hydrogen Hubs' to foster integrated production and consumption ecosystems.
• Incentives include tax breaks, subsidized land, and priority access to renewable energy for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
• The move seeks to position India as a global leader in green hydrogen, focusing on reducing carbon emissions and achieving energy independence.
• Special emphasis is placed on developing infrastructure for storage, transport, and export of green hydrogen and its derivatives like green ammonia.
gsSubTopics: Energy
Infrastructure: Energy
Environmental pollution & degradation
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission launched in 2023 with an outlay of ₹19,744 crore.
SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme is a major component, supporting electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
Green Hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water using renewable energy.
India aims to produce 5 million tonnes of green hydrogen annually by 2030.
Policy focuses on 'Green Hydrogen Hubs' for integrated ecosystem development.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's strategy for achieving energy independence and mitigating climate change through green hydrogen.
Analyze the economic and environmental benefits of developing 'Green Hydrogen Hubs' and their potential for job creation and exports.
Examine the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production, including cost, infrastructure, and technological advancements.
Evaluate the role of government policies and incentives in fostering a competitive green hydrogen ecosystem in India.
Assess the geopolitical implications of India becoming a major green hydrogen producer and exporter.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration of a green hydrogen production facility with solar panels and wind turbines in the background.
GS3.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Easing Inflationary Pressures, Focus on Growth
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) unanimously decided to keep the repo rate unchanged at 6.5% for the sixth consecutive review.
• This decision comes as retail inflation shows signs of moderation, albeit remaining above the MPC's comfort zone, while global economic uncertainties persist.
• The RBI reiterated its commitment to 'withdrawal of accommodation' to ensure inflation aligns with the target while supporting economic growth.
• GDP growth projection for FY26 was revised marginally upwards, signaling cautious optimism regarding economic recovery.
• The MPC highlighted the importance of a stable financial system and continues to monitor liquidity conditions closely.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary Policy
Inflation
Growth
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) consists of six members: three from RBI and three external members appointed by the government.
The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Retail inflation target is 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
Withdrawal of accommodation refers to reducing surplus liquidity in the system and raising interest rates to curb inflation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind the RBI's decision to maintain the status quo on the repo rate and its implications for the Indian economy.
Discuss the challenges faced by the MPC in balancing inflation control with the objective of fostering economic growth.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the RBI's monetary policy tools in managing liquidity and credit conditions.
Examine the impact of global economic uncertainties and geopolitical developments on India's monetary policy decisions.
Suggest reforms to strengthen the autonomy and effectiveness of the Monetary Policy Committee.
GS3.imageDescription: RBI logo with graphs showing economic trends in the background.
GS3.trivia: The RBI was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934, and was nationalized in 1949.

GS3.headline: National Cyber Security Strategy 2.0 Focuses on Critical Infrastructure Protection and Skill Development
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) unveiled the National Cyber Security Strategy 2.0, updating the previous framework from 2013.
• The new strategy prioritizes securing critical information infrastructure (CII) across sectors like energy, finance, and telecommunications.
• It emphasizes building a robust cyber workforce through skill development programs and public-private partnerships.
• Key pillars include active cyber defence, resilience building, incident response mechanisms, and international cooperation.
• The strategy also addresses emerging threats from AI, quantum computing, and supply chain vulnerabilities, aiming for a 'secure and resilient' cyberspace.
gsSubTopics: Internal security challenges
Cyber security
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
GS3.source: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY)
keyTerms: NCIIPC (National Critical Information Infrastructure Protection Centre)
prelimsPoints: The first National Cyber Security Policy was launched in 2013.
CERT-In is the national agency for incident response and threat intelligence.
NCIIPC is responsible for protecting critical information infrastructure.
The new strategy addresses emerging technologies like AI, IoT, and quantum computing.
Focus areas include supply chain security, cyber deterrence, and international collaboration.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key features and objectives of India's National Cyber Security Strategy 2.0 and its significance in the evolving digital landscape.
Discuss the challenges in protecting critical information infrastructure and the measures proposed in the strategy to address them.
Evaluate the role of public-private partnerships and skill development in building a robust cyber workforce and enhancing national cyber resilience.
Examine the implications of emerging technologies like AI and quantum computing on cybersecurity and India's preparedness.
Suggest policy recommendations for strengthening international cooperation in combating trans-border cyber threats.
GS3.imageDescription: Abstract image of a digital shield protecting a network grid.
GS3.trivia: India ranks among the top 10 countries globally in the ITU's Global Cybersecurity Index.

GS3.headline: NCAP Phase-II: Government Launches New Initiatives for City-Specific Air Pollution Reduction Targets
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) has inaugurated Phase-II of the National Clean Air Programme (NCAP).
• This phase introduces more stringent, city-specific targets for reducing particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10) concentrations by 20-30% by 2029, building on earlier goals.
• New initiatives include enhanced deployment of advanced air quality monitoring stations and real-time data dissemination platforms.
• Focus will be on source apportionment studies in additional non-attainment cities and promoting electric vehicles and clean energy.
• The program aims for a holistic approach, integrating urban planning, industrial emission control, and waste management strategies.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
GS3.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
keyTerms: System of Air Quality and Weather Forecasting and Research (SAFAR)
prelimsPoints: NCAP was launched in 2019 to reduce particulate matter concentration by 20-30% by 2024 from 2017 levels.
It covers 131 non-attainment cities across India.
PM2.5 and PM10 are key indicators of air quality.
Source apportionment studies identify major contributors to air pollution in a specific area.
SAFAR provides real-time air quality information and forecasts.
mainsPoints: Assess the effectiveness of NCAP Phase-I in improving urban air quality and the lessons learned for Phase-II.
Discuss the multi-faceted challenges in combating air pollution in Indian cities, including vehicular emissions, industrial discharge, and agricultural burning.
Evaluate the new initiatives under NCAP Phase-II, such as city-specific targets and advanced monitoring, for their potential impact.
Examine the role of inter-ministerial coordination and public participation in achieving clean air goals.
Suggest sustainable urban planning and policy interventions required to address the root causes of air pollution.
GS3.imageDescription: City skyline shrouded in smog with a clean air technology superimposed.
GS3.trivia: Globally, air pollution is responsible for millions of premature deaths each year, making it one of the largest environmental health risks.

GS3.headline: India Pitches for Global Framework on Ethical AI Governance at UN General Assembly Side Event
GS3.explainer:
• India, at a side event during the 80th UN General Assembly session, advocated for a robust international framework for ethical AI governance.
• The Indian delegation highlighted the need for AI development to be responsible, inclusive, and transparent, ensuring it benefits all humanity.
• Discussions focused on mitigating risks associated with AI, such as algorithmic bias, privacy violations, and autonomous weapons systems.
• India emphasized its 'AI for All' approach, promoting equitable access and capacity building for developing nations.
• The initiative seeks to build consensus among member states on common principles and norms for AI regulation and deployment.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
IT & Computers
Important International Institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
prelimsPoints: UN General Assembly's 80th session is ongoing in September 2025.
India has been advocating for multi-stakeholder and democratic governance of emerging technologies.
Ethical AI principles include fairness, accountability, transparency, and privacy.
Algorithmic bias refers to systematic and repeatable errors in a computer system's output that create unfair outcomes.
India's national AI strategy promotes 'AI for All' for inclusive growth.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's rationale for advocating a global framework on ethical AI governance and its potential impact on international relations.
Discuss the key ethical challenges posed by Artificial Intelligence, including bias, privacy, and accountability.
Evaluate the 'AI for All' approach and its relevance for developing countries in harnessing AI benefits while mitigating risks.
Examine the role of international organizations like the UN in shaping norms and regulations for emerging technologies.
Suggest how India can leverage its technological expertise and diplomatic influence to lead global discussions on responsible AI development.
GS3.imageDescription: Globe with interconnected AI neural networks, symbolizing global governance.
GS3.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined in 1956 by John McCarthy.

GS3.headline: Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme Extended to Cover Advanced Battery Manufacturing and Textiles
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved the expansion of the Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme to include new sectors: Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC) Battery Manufacturing and Textiles.
• This move aims to boost domestic manufacturing capabilities, attract global investments, and enhance India's export competitiveness in these strategic sectors.
• For ACC batteries, the scheme targets reducing import dependence for electric vehicles and energy storage solutions.
• In textiles, the PLI scheme focuses on promoting the production of Man-Made Fibre (MMF) apparel and technical textiles.
• The expansion is part of the 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiative, designed to integrate India into global supply chains and create employment opportunities.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth
Infrastructure: Energy
prelimsPoints: The PLI scheme was introduced in 2020 to boost domestic manufacturing and exports.
It provides incentives on incremental sales for products manufactured in India.
Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC) batteries are crucial for electric vehicles and grid-scale energy storage.
Technical textiles are functional fabrics used in diverse industries like automotive, medical, and agriculture.
The scheme currently covers 14 key sectors, with these additions making it 16.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and expected outcomes of extending the PLI scheme to Advanced Battery Manufacturing and Textiles.
Discuss how the PLI scheme contributes to India's 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' vision and its goal of becoming a global manufacturing hub.
Evaluate the potential challenges and opportunities for these new sectors under the PLI framework.
Examine the fiscal implications of the PLI scheme and its impact on government finances and industrial growth.
Suggest measures to enhance the effectiveness and broaden the scope of the PLI scheme to other critical sectors.
GS3.imageDescription: Factory floor with robots assembling battery cells, and a textile loom in the background.
GS3.trivia: India is the world's second-largest producer of textiles and garments.

GS3.headline: New Study Highlights Accelerated Retreat of Himalayan Glaciers, Urges Urgent Climate Action
GS3.explainer:
• A comprehensive study published by the Wadia Institute of Himalayan Geology reveals an alarming rate of retreat for glaciers across the Himalayan region.
• The research, utilizing satellite imagery and field data, indicates that glaciers are melting at twice the rate observed in the late 20th century.
• This accelerated melting poses severe threats to water security for millions in South Asia, increases the risk of Glacier Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs), and impacts regional ecosystems.
• Scientists attribute the rapid retreat primarily to rising global temperatures and localized black carbon deposition.
• The study calls for immediate and concerted efforts in climate change mitigation and adaptation strategies across the Himalayan nations.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution & degradation
Disaster and disaster management

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes
prelimsPoints: The Himalayas are often called the 'Third Pole' due to their vast ice reserves outside the polar regions.
Glacier Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) are sudden releases of water from glacier lakes.
Black carbon, a component of particulate matter, accelerates glacier melt by absorbing sunlight.
The Indus, Ganga, and Brahmaputra rivers are fed by Himalayan glaciers.
The Wadia Institute of Himalayan Geology is an autonomous research organization under the Department of Science & Technology, Govt. of India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the causes and consequences of accelerated Himalayan glacier retreat on water resources, ecosystems, and human livelihoods in South Asia.
Discuss the immediate and long-term risks posed by Glacier Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) and the strategies for disaster risk reduction.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current climate change mitigation and adaptation policies in protecting the Himalayan region.
Examine the role of regional cooperation among Himalayan nations in addressing transboundary impacts of glacier melt.
Suggest sustainable development practices and policy interventions to build resilience in communities dependent on Himalayan ecosystems.
GS3.imageDescription: Melting glacier in the Himalayas with visible water runoff.
GS3.trivia: The Himalayas contain the world's third-largest deposit of ice and snow, after the Arctic and Antarctic.

GS3.headline: IIT-Bombay Researchers Develop Novel Solid-State Battery for Enhanced Renewable Energy Storage
GS3.explainer:
• Scientists at IIT-Bombay have announced a significant breakthrough in battery technology with the development of a novel solid-state battery.
• The new battery chemistry promises higher energy density, faster charging capabilities, and enhanced safety compared to conventional lithium-ion batteries.
• This innovation is particularly geared towards efficient storage solutions for renewable energy sources like solar and wind power, and for electric vehicles.
• The research focuses on using indigenous materials, reducing dependence on critical imported raw materials.
• The team is now working on scaling up the technology for commercial production and integration into India's energy ecosystem.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Infrastructure: Energy
prelimsPoints: Solid-state batteries use a solid electrolyte instead of the liquid or polymer gel electrolyte found in conventional lithium-ion batteries.
They offer advantages like higher energy density, longer lifespan, and improved safety (less prone to thermal runaway).
Lithium-ion batteries are widely used in portable electronics and electric vehicles.
Indigenous development of battery technology is crucial for India's energy security and EV roadmap.
IIT Bombay is one of India's premier technical institutes.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of solid-state battery technology for India's energy transition and electric vehicle ecosystem.
Analyze the advantages and challenges of developing and commercializing indigenous battery technologies.
Evaluate the potential impact of this breakthrough on renewable energy storage and grid stability.
Examine the role of academic research and innovation in addressing critical technological needs for national development.
Suggest policy measures to facilitate the translation of laboratory breakthroughs into large-scale industrial applications.
GS3.imageDescription: Microscopic view of battery components with energy flow lines.
GS3.trivia: The first battery, the voltaic pile, was invented by Alessandro Volta in 1800.

sources: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY)
Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
